title: Livonia, Avon and Lakeville Railroad

Please help [improve this article](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Livonia,_Avon_and_Lakeville_Railroad "Special:EditPage/Livonia, Avon and Lakeville Railroad") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners"). Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *Find sources:* ["Livonia, Avon and Lakeville Railroad"](https://www.google.com/search?as_eq=wikipedia&q=%22Livonia%2C+Avon+and+Lakeville+Railroad%22) – [news](https://www.google.com/search?tbm=nws&q=%22Livonia%2C+Avon+and+Lakeville+Railroad%22+-wikipedia&tbs=ar:1) **·** [newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22Livonia%2C+Avon+and+Lakeville+Railroad%22&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22Livonia%2C+Avon+and+Lakeville+Railroad%22+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22Livonia%2C+Avon+and+Lakeville+Railroad%22) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22Livonia%2C+Avon+and+Lakeville+Railroad%22&acc=on&wc=on) *(April 2024)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))*

[newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22Livonia%2C+Avon+and+Lakeville+Railroad%22&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22Livonia%2C+Avon+and+Lakeville+Railroad%22+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22Livonia%2C+Avon+and+Lakeville+Railroad%22) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22Livonia%2C+Avon+and+Lakeville+Railroad%22&acc=on&wc=on) *(April 2024)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

([reporting mark](/wiki/Reporting_mark "Reporting mark") **LAL**) is a [short line railroad](/wiki/Short_line_railroad "Short line railroad") that operates in [Livingston County](/wiki/Livingston_County,_New_York "Livingston County, New York") and [Monroe County](/wiki/Monroe_County,_New_York "Monroe County, New York") in [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)"), United States. The railroad interchanges with [CSX](/wiki/CSX_Transportation "CSX Transportation") at [Genesee Junction](/wiki/Genesee_Junction "Genesee Junction") in [Chili, New York](/wiki/Chili,_New_York "Chili, New York"), the [Rochester and Southern Railroad](/wiki/Rochester_and_Southern_Railroad "Rochester and Southern Railroad") (RSR) at Genesee Junction and the RSR's Brooks Avenue Yard in [Gates, New York](/wiki/Gates,_New_York "Gates, New York"), and with the [Rochester & Genesee Valley Railroad Museum](/wiki/Rochester_%26_Genesee_Valley_Railroad_Museum "Rochester & Genesee Valley Railroad Museum") at [Industry, New York](/wiki/Industry,_New_York "Industry, New York"). Their primary freight consists of food products: [grains](/wiki/Grain "Grain") and [corn syrup](/wiki/Corn_syrup "Corn syrup"). In 1997, the Livonia, Avon and Lakeville Railroad was selected as [Short Line Railroad of the Year](/wiki/Short_Line_Railroad_of_the_Year "Short Line Railroad of the Year") by industry trade journal [Railway Age](/wiki/Railway_Age "Railway Age"). The LAL is also the parent company for the [Bath and Hammondsport Railroad](/wiki/Bath_and_Hammondsport_Railroad "Bath and Hammondsport Railroad"), the [Western New York and Pennsylvania Railroad](/wiki/Western_New_York_and_Pennsylvania_Railroad_(2001) "Western New York and Pennsylvania Railroad (2001)") and the [Ontario Midland Railroad](/wiki/Ontario_Midland_Railroad "Ontario Midland Railroad").

Railroad in New York, United States

Livonia, Avon and Lakeville Railroad

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | |
|  | |
| Overview | |
| Headquarters | [Lakeville, New York](/wiki/Lakeville,_New_York "Lakeville, New York") |
| [Reporting mark](/wiki/Reporting_mark "Reporting mark") | LAL |
| Locale | [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)") |
| Dates of operation | 1964–present |
| Technical | |
| [Track gauge](/wiki/Track_gauge "Track gauge") | 4 ft 8+1⁄2 in (1,435 mm) [standard gauge](/wiki/Standard_gauge "Standard gauge") |
| Length | 400 miles (640 km) |
| Other | |
| Website | <http://www.lalrr.com/> |

The **Livonia, Avon and Lakeville Railroad** ([reporting mark](/wiki/Reporting_mark "Reporting mark") **LAL**) is a [short line railroad](/wiki/Short_line_railroad "Short line railroad") that operates in [Livingston County](/wiki/Livingston_County,_New_York "Livingston County, New York") and [Monroe County](/wiki/Monroe_County,_New_York "Monroe County, New York") in [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)"), United States. The railroad interchanges with [CSX](/wiki/CSX_Transportation "CSX Transportation") at [Genesee Junction](/wiki/Genesee_Junction "Genesee Junction") in [Chili, New York](/wiki/Chili,_New_York "Chili, New York"), the [Rochester and Southern Railroad](/wiki/Rochester_and_Southern_Railroad "Rochester and Southern Railroad") (RSR) at Genesee Junction and the RSR's Brooks Avenue Yard in [Gates, New York](/wiki/Gates,_New_York "Gates, New York"), and with the [Rochester & Genesee Valley Railroad Museum](/wiki/Rochester_%26_Genesee_Valley_Railroad_Museum "Rochester & Genesee Valley Railroad Museum") at [Industry, New York](/wiki/Industry,_New_York "Industry, New York"). Their primary freight consists of food products: [grains](/wiki/Grain "Grain") and [corn syrup](/wiki/Corn_syrup "Corn syrup"). In 1997, the Livonia, Avon and Lakeville Railroad was selected as [Short Line Railroad of the Year](/wiki/Short_Line_Railroad_of_the_Year "Short Line Railroad of the Year") by industry trade journal [Railway Age](/wiki/Railway_Age "Railway Age"). The LAL is also the parent company for the [Bath and Hammondsport Railroad](/wiki/Bath_and_Hammondsport_Railroad "Bath and Hammondsport Railroad"), the [Western New York and Pennsylvania Railroad](/wiki/Western_New_York_and_Pennsylvania_Railroad_(2001) "Western New York and Pennsylvania Railroad (2001)") and the [Ontario Midland Railroad](/wiki/Ontario_Midland_Railroad "Ontario Midland Railroad").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Livonia,_Avon_and_Lakeville_Railroad&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### The beginning

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Livonia,_Avon_and_Lakeville_Railroad&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: The beginning")]

The route that is now the Livonia, Avon & Lakeville started in the mid-19th century. Originally, the line from Mortimer to Avon was part of the Rochester & Genesee Valley Railroad – which had constructed an 18-mile route from downtown [Rochester, New York](/wiki/Rochester,_New_York "Rochester, New York"), to [Avon, New York](/wiki/Avon,_New_York "Avon, New York"), by 1851.[[1]](#cite_note-1) Eventually this became part of the [Erie Railroad](/wiki/Erie_Railroad "Erie Railroad"), then part of the [Erie-Lackawanna Railroad](/wiki/Erie-Lackawanna_Railroad "Erie-Lackawanna Railroad") in 1960, when the Erie merged with the [Delaware, Lackawanna and Western Railroad](/wiki/Delaware,_Lackawanna_and_Western_Railroad "Delaware, Lackawanna and Western Railroad").

The Livonia Avon & Lakeville Railroad was born when members of the Livonia community decided to rescue the railroad after news came that the Erie-Lackawanna Railroad was to abandon its spur south from [Avon](/wiki/Avon,_New_York "Avon, New York") connecting [Lakeville](/wiki/Lakeville,_New_York "Lakeville, New York") and [Livonia](/wiki/Livonia_(village),_New_York "Livonia (village), New York"). In 1964, members of the community, led by Chester A. Haak and Harry J. Moran, began a campaign to purchase the soon to be abandoned line from the Erie-Lackawanna for $13,000.

After its incorporation in May 1964, passenger excursions began with the use of [GE 45-Tonner](/wiki/GE_45-ton_switcher "GE 45-ton switcher") #97. This engine was sold a year later,[[2]](#cite_note-2) and replaced by [GE 44-tonner](/wiki/GE_44-ton_switcher "GE 44-ton switcher") #10. The next year, after acquiring #17, a [2-8-2](/wiki/2-8-2 "2-8-2") [steam locomotive](/wiki/Steam_locomotive "Steam locomotive") and a former [Savannah and Atlanta Railway](/wiki/Savannah_and_Atlanta_Railway "Savannah and Atlanta Railway") locomotive, the LA&L used this engine for all passenger excursion operations with the old Erie depot in Livonia as excursion headquarters. Three years later, #17 began to develop mechanical issues, and was replaced with #[38](/wiki/Huntingdon_and_Broad_Top_38 "Huntingdon and Broad Top 38"), a [2-8-0](/wiki/2-8-0 "2-8-0") steam locomotive. #17 was later sold to a Rochester businessman who sold it for scrap. In 1972, the railroad acquired its first [ALCO](/wiki/ALCO "ALCO") [diesel locomotive](/wiki/Diesel_locomotive "Diesel locomotive") #20, an [ALCO RS-1](/wiki/ALCO_RS-1 "ALCO RS-1"). Three years after this, in 1975, #38 was stored out of service in need of major boiler repairs.

### Conrail years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Livonia,_Avon_and_Lakeville_Railroad&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Conrail years")]

On April 1, 1976, six major railroads in the [northeast United States](/wiki/Northeast_United_States "Northeast United States") became one, [Conrail](/wiki/Conrail "Conrail"). Conrail was now the interchange railroad in Avon, NY, as opposed to the EL. During the planning of Conrail, the LA&L petitioned the [United States Railroad Administration](/wiki/United_States_Railroad_Administration "United States Railroad Administration") to give them the Avon-Caledonia branch of the EL which would have allowed them to interchange with the [Chessie System](/wiki/Chessie_System "Chessie System") in [Caledonia](/wiki/Caledonia_(village),_New_York "Caledonia (village), New York"), as well as Conrail. However, the line was excluded from Conrail and later abandoned. In 1977, growing insurance costs forced the LA&L to discontinue passenger excursions.

1980 saw the LAL purchasing their second ALCO, #72, an [Alco S2](/wiki/Alco_S2 "Alco S2"), followed by the selling of the #10. In 1981, the line to Livonia was abandoned due to a lack of support from the state of New York to repair an aging bridge. After the abandonment, the LA&L built a team track in Lakeville to service its customers. In this year, the LA&L also acquired another ALCO locomotive, this time a [C-425](/wiki/ALCO_Century_425 "ALCO Century 425"), numbered 425. When 1982 rolled around it was decided that a [classification yard](/wiki/Classification_yard "Classification yard") was needed in Lakeville to handle the growing amount of traffic that the railroad was receiving. Later in 1988, this five track yard was completed. Two years later, an engine shop and office building were built next to the yard.

In 1995, the LA&L acquired yet another engine, an [ALCO C-420](/wiki/ALCO_Century_420 "ALCO Century 420") numbered 420. The shop was extended to hold two coupled road engines.

In 1996, the LAL acquired Conrail's "Rochester South Cluster" pursuant to the [Interstate Commerce Commission](/wiki/Interstate_Commerce_Commission "Interstate Commerce Commission") Termination Act of 1995. Instead of interchanging in Avon, the LAL now operated north to [Genesee Junction](/wiki/Genesee_Junction "Genesee Junction"), where they would interchange with Conrail. The railroad launched a $1.4 million rehabilitation effort.

In the same year, the LA&L began operation of the former [Champagne Railroad](/w/index.php?title=Champagne_Railroad&action=edit&redlink=1 "Champagne Railroad (page does not exist)"), now known as the [Bath and Hammondsport Railroad](/wiki/Bath_and_Hammondsport_Railroad "Bath and Hammondsport Railroad"), previously owned and operated by the [Steuben County Industrial Development Agency](http://www.steubencountyida.com/).[[3]](#cite_note-3) With this deal, the LA&L inherited the use of the railroad's two remaining [ALCO S1](/wiki/ALCO_S1 "ALCO S1") engines, numbers 4 and 5, to service the newly acquired railroad. With this acquisition, the railroad grew to 65 route miles. Also, four [ALCO C-424m](/wiki/ALCO_Century_424 "ALCO Century 424") locomotives were purchased. The LA&L was also named "Industry of the Year" by the [Livingston County Chamber of Commerce](http://www.livingstoncountychamber.com/).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/30/LAL_420_and_425_at_East_River_Rd.jpg/250px-LAL_420_and_425_at_East_River_Rd.jpg)](/wiki/File:LAL_420_and_425_at_East_River_Rd.jpg)

LAL 420 & 425 headed north to Genesee Junction.

### Continued expansion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Livonia,_Avon_and_Lakeville_Railroad&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Continued expansion")]

In 1997, [CSX](/wiki/CSX "CSX") and [Norfolk Southern Railway](/wiki/Norfolk_Southern_Railway "Norfolk Southern Railway") applied to split Conrail, the LA&L filed an application to remove the paper barrier that had kept the railroad from interchanging with the Rochester & Southern at [Genesee Junction](/wiki/Genesee_Junction "Genesee Junction"). With fierce opposition from CSX, the [Surface Transportation Board](/wiki/Surface_Transportation_Board "Surface Transportation Board") granted[[4]](#cite_note-4) the LA&L [trackage rights](/wiki/Trackage_rights "Trackage rights") across the Genesee Junction Yard, trackage rights into the R&S and its yard at Brooks Avenue, and haulage rights on the R&S to [Silver Springs, New York](/wiki/Silver_Springs,_New_York "Silver Springs, New York"). This gave the LA&L access to three [Class I railroads](/wiki/Class_I_railroad "Class I railroad"): CSX, Norfolk Southern, and [Canadian Pacific Railway](/wiki/Canadian_Pacific_Railway "Canadian Pacific Railway").

1998 was a busy year for the LAL. A number of infrastructure rehabilitation projects resulted in the line from Lakeville to Genesee Jct becoming a 25MPH railroad – not the 5-10MPH that had been. The B&H also saw improvements on the northern end of the line, including the reactivation of ten miles of previously unused rail between Cohocton and [Wayland, NY](/wiki/Wayland,_NY "Wayland, NY"). New track was also built to serve a new road de-icer processing facility. Also in this year, the LA&L gained a controlling interest in the [Ontario Central Railroad](/wiki/Ontario_Central_Railroad "Ontario Central Railroad").[[5]](#cite_note-5) Due to the Conrail split, the ONCT finally received competitive connections to CSX, NS, CP Rail, and [NYSW](/wiki/NYSW "NYSW") by way of the [Finger Lakes Railway](/wiki/Finger_Lakes_Railway "Finger Lakes Railway").

During 1999, the bridge on Pole Bridge Road was upgraded to handle 286,000 pound [railroad cars](/wiki/Railroad_cars "Railroad cars"). Avon Yard was also rebuilt into its current configuration. The Lakeville shop was expanded to accommodate three coupled road engines with additional floor space in a separate bay to facilitate locomotive repair. Two of the ALCO C-424m locomotives purchased in 1995 were repaired and reactivated, emerging as numbers 423 and 424.

In 2001, the LA&L began operations on the former Erie-Lackawanna mainline between [Hornell, New York](/wiki/Hornell,_New_York "Hornell, New York"), and [Corry, Pennsylvania](/wiki/Corry,_Pennsylvania "Corry, Pennsylvania"), as the [Western New York and Pennsylvania Railroad](/wiki/Western_New_York_and_Pennsylvania_Railroad_(2001) "Western New York and Pennsylvania Railroad (2001)").[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7) The railroad initially provided rail service between Olean and [Jamestown, New York](/wiki/Jamestown,_New_York "Jamestown, New York"), but eventually extended to more of the line as out-of-service sections were reactivated. In October of that year, the Cohocton Valley Rail Corp. was renamed as the Bath & Hammondsport Rail Corp. Subsidiary WNYP leased additional trackage from Norfolk Southern in 2005 extending from [Meadville, PA](/wiki/Meadville,_PA "Meadville, PA") to [Oil City, PA](/wiki/Oil_City,_PA "Oil City, PA").

In 2007, the STB granted an exemption to [Finger Lakes Railway](/wiki/Finger_Lakes_Railway "Finger Lakes Railway") allowing them to acquire the Ontario Central from LAL.[[8]](#cite_note-8) With the completion of the transaction, ONCT RS-36 418 was permanently transferred from LAL. From May through September, the LAL was busy upgrading the majority of its 30-mile mainline from 105 Lbs [jointed rail](/wiki/Rail_tracks#Jointed_track "Rail tracks") to 127Lbs [welded rail](/wiki/Rail_tracks#Continuous_welded_rail "Rail tracks") - allowing faster trains with heavier loads. Additionally, in 2007 a new [Barilla Pasta](/wiki/Barilla_Pasta "Barilla Pasta") plant opened in Avon, which receives grains via rail. According to Barilla's US top executive, Kirk Trofholz, the availability of rail transport was "a must" when choosing the location for the plant.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

The WNYP saw massive expansion, with the leasing of NS tracks from [Machias, New York](/wiki/Machias,_New_York "Machias, New York"), south through Olean, NY, to [Driftwood, PA](/wiki/Driftwood,_PA "Driftwood, PA").[[10]](#cite_note-10) Along with the trackage, the company purchased or leased eight 6-axle Alcos.

On May 9, 2015, the LA&L celebrated its 50th anniversary. Excursion trains were run between [Lakeville, New York](/wiki/Lakeville,_New_York "Lakeville, New York"), and [Henrietta, New York](/wiki/Henrietta,_New_York "Henrietta, New York"), followed by a night photo shoot of the locomotives to commemorate the milestone.

On September 16, 2022, the Livonia, Avon & Lakeville Railroad acquired the controlling stake of Ontario Midland Railroad by purchasing 55% of its stock according to paperwork filed with the Surface Transportation Board. The OMID, based in [Sodus, New York](/wiki/Sodus,_New_York "Sodus, New York"), operates 52 miles (84 km) of track in [Wayne](/wiki/Wayne_County,_New_York "Wayne County, New York") and [Monroe](/wiki/Monroe_County,_New_York "Monroe County, New York") counties.[[11]](#cite_note-R&R-11)[[12]](#cite_note-RT-12)[[13]](#cite_note-RA-13)

## Locomotive roster

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Livonia,_Avon_and_Lakeville_Railroad&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Locomotive roster")]

The Livonia, Avon and Lakeville Railroad has a roster made up of primarily [ALCO](/wiki/American_Locomotive_Company "American Locomotive Company") locomotives. The LA&L rosters an [S-2](/wiki/ALCO_S-2 "ALCO S-2") (#72) built in 1941, an [RS-1](/wiki/ALCO_RS-1 "ALCO RS-1") (#20) built in 1949, two [S-1s](/wiki/ALCO_S-1 "ALCO S-1") (#4 and 5), one [C-420](/wiki/ALCO_Century_420 "ALCO Century 420") (#420), seven [C-424s](/wiki/ALCO_Century_424 "ALCO Century 424") (#421, 422, 423, 424, 426, 427, 428), four of which are of the C-424m rebuilt variant, one [C-425](/wiki/ALCO_Century_425 "ALCO Century 425") (#425), four [C-430s](/wiki/ALCO_Century_430 "ALCO Century 430") (#430, 431, 432, 433), one [C-630](/wiki/ALCO_Century_630 "ALCO Century 630") (#630), one [M-630](/wiki/ALCO_Century_630 "ALCO Century 630") (#631), and an [RS-36](/wiki/ALCO_RS-36 "ALCO RS-36") (#418). Several of the locomotives are lettered for LA&L subsidiaries Bath & Hammondsport or Western New York & Pennsylvania.

Before the road became an all-Alco road, their roster consisted of a [Baldwin](/wiki/Baldwin_Locomotive_Works "Baldwin Locomotive Works") 2-8-2 (#17), a GE 45 Tonner with side rods (#97), and in later years, one GE 44 Tonner (#10) and one 2-8-0 (#[38](/wiki/Huntingdon_and_Broad_Top_38 "Huntingdon and Broad Top 38")). Number 17 was scrapped in Lakeville after being sold to a Rochester area business-man. Number 38 survives on the [Everett Railroad](/wiki/Everett_Railroad "Everett Railroad") at Duncansville, PA. Former #97 was later sold to [Rochester Gas and Electric](/wiki/Rochester_Gas_and_Electric "Rochester Gas and Electric"), where it was used to switch coal cars at Bee Bee Station. When Bee Bee station closed in the early 1990s, the locomotive was donated to the [Rochester & Genesee Valley Railroad Museum](/wiki/Rochester_%26_Genesee_Valley_Railroad_Museum "Rochester & Genesee Valley Railroad Museum"), where it has been restored to operating condition as [RGE #1941](http://rgvrrm.org/about/railroad/rge1941).

## Trackage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Livonia,_Avon_and_Lakeville_Railroad&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Trackage")]

The LAL has trackage rights at CSX's Genesee Junction Yard, allowing the LAL to interchange with the Rochester and Southern Railroad as well as CSX. LAL ownership begins at the east end of Genesee Junction with the former [Conrail](/wiki/Conrail "Conrail") non-controlled siding that runs parallel to the West Shore Branch from Genesee Junction Yard to MP 360. There, the line heads south onto what was once [Erie-Lackawanna Railroad](/wiki/Erie-Lackawanna_Railroad "Erie-Lackawanna Railroad") and [Lehigh Valley Railroad](/wiki/Lehigh_Valley_Railroad "Lehigh Valley Railroad") trackage. Shortly after crossing Jefferson Road ([NY-252](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_252 "New York State Route 252")), the track splits, with one track, the ex-LV Rochester Branch, heading southeast to a pair of online businesses on Lehigh Station Road ([NY-253](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_253 "New York State Route 253")) before coming to an end, while the other track, ex-EL trackage, continues south/southwest towards Avon and eventually hits the Lakeville terminus, ending just shy of [Conesus Lake](/wiki/Conesus_Lake "Conesus Lake"), at one time a Steam Boat terminal.

In Avon, there is a short branch to [Kraft Foods](/wiki/Kraft_Foods "Kraft Foods"), which is the only remaining part of the Erie's branch to [Mount Morris, New York](/wiki/Mount_Morris,_New_York "Mount Morris, New York"). In 1994 the LAL reactivated a spur to a grain elevator on Bronson Hill Road. This spur was formerly part of the Livonia Branch of the LAL which had been abandoned in 1981.

The LAL has a three-track yard in the town of [Avon](/wiki/Avon_(town),_New_York "Avon (town), New York") which was expanded in 2008 due to increased traffic to [Barilla Pasta](/wiki/Barilla_Pasta "Barilla Pasta")'s manufacturing plant in Avon which opened in 2007. Lakeville is the site of a five-track class-yard and a four-track engine house as well as [ADM](/wiki/Archer_Daniels_Midland "Archer Daniels Midland") and [Sweeteners Plus](/w/index.php?title=Sweeteners_Plus&action=edit&redlink=1 "Sweeteners Plus (page does not exist)").
